perturbation.original_text: Within five (5) business days following receipt of reasonably satisfactory documentation thereof, the Company will reimburse the MG Capital Parties for their fees and expenses (including all legal, public relations, proxy advisory and out-of-pocket expenses, the "Expenses") incurred in preparation for and in connection with the matters relating to the consent solicitation run by the MG Capital Parties, the 2020 Annual Meeting and the negotiation, execution and effectuation of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, in an amount equal to $352,290.25 (the "Initial Reimbursement"). Between the date of the Initial Reimbursement and the date of the 2020 Annual Meeting, the Company will reimburse the MG Capital Parties for the Expenses in an aggregate amount not exceeding $650,000 (inclusive of the Initial Reimbursement, the "Cap"), which Expenses shall be reimbursed on a dollar-for-dollar basis at the same time as the Company reimburses its third party vendors (e.g., legal counsel, public relations firm, financial advisor and proxy advisory firm)in connection with the MG Capital Parties' consent solicitation and Nomination Notice; provided that all Expenses, subject to the Cap, shall be reimbursed no later than the date of the 2020 Annual Meeting.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will reimburse the MG Capital Parties for their fees and expenses (including all legal, public relations, proxy advisory and out-of-pocket expenses, the "Expenses") incurred in preparation for and in connection with the matters relating to the consent solicitation run by the MG Capital Parties, the 2020 Annual Meeting and the negotiation, execution and effectuation of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, in an amount equal to $352,290.25 (the "Initial Reimbursement") at the Company's discretion. Between the date of the Initial Reimbursement and the date of the 2020 Annual Meeting, the Company will reimburse the MG Capital Parties for the Expenses in an aggregate amount not exceeding $650,000 (inclusive of the Initial Reimbursement, the "Cap"), which Expenses shall be reimbursed only if the Company's financial condition permits. There is no fixed deadline for reimbursement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the defined timeline and stating that reimbursement is at the company's discretion creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance with general contract law principles that require clarity and definiteness in payment terms. It also violates the principle of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law, where HC2 is incorporated, implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This means parties must act honestly and reasonably. Removing the reimbursement deadline and making it conditional on the company's discretion could be interpreted as a violation of this covenant, as it allows the company to potentially avoid its obligation without a legitimate reason. The modification undermines the reasonable expectations of MG Capital Parties, who agreed to the deal expecting timely reimbursement of expenses.

(a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this State and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this State or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the reimbursement obligation by removing the fixed timeline and introducing company discretion, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on the Uniform Commercial Code and territorial applicability, it also mentions that 'the obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement' indicating the importance of good faith. By making reimbursement conditional and discretionary, the modified text allows the company to avoid its obligation without legitimate reason, which contradicts the reasonable expectations of MG Capital Parties.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will terminate upon the earlier of: (i) the conclusion of the Standstill Period or (ii) delivery of written notice by one Party to the other Party of a material breach of this Agreement by the breaching Party that is uncured after ten (10) calendar days of notice of such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will terminate upon the earlier of: (i) the conclusion of the Standstill Period or (ii) delivery of written notice by one Party to the other Party of a material breach of this Agreement by the breaching Party that is uncured after two (2) calendar days of notice of such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to just two calendar days may be considered unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the cure period to two days may not provide the breaching party with a reasonable opportunity to rectify the breach, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law. A court might find that such a short period is unconscionable, especially given the complexities involved.

perturbation.justification: The change from ten days to two days to cure a material breach introduces a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that such a short cure period may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, section 1-302(b), states that 'The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement.' By reducing the cure period to two days, the modified text potentially undermines the 'reasonableness' aspect of good faith, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly following the execution of this Agreement, the Company shall issue a joint press release with the MG Capital Parties substantially in the form attached to this Agreement as Exhibit B (the "Press Release"), with such modifications, if any, as may be mutually agreed between the Company and the MG Capital Parties, and (i) the Company shall file a Current Report on Form 8-K, which shall be in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the Company and the MG Capital Parties (for the avoidance of doubt, nothing herein shall prohibit the Company from complying with its obligation to file such Current Report by the deadline therefor) and (ii) the MG Capital Parties shall file an amendment to its Schedule 13D announcing this Agreement and the withdrawal of the 2020 Consent Solicitation, which shall be in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the Company and the MG Capital Parties (for the avoidance of doubt, nothing herein shall prohibit the MG Capital Parties from complying with its obligation to file such amendment by the deadline therefor).
perturbation.changed_text: At its earliest convenience, the Company may consider whether to issue a joint press release with the MG Capital Parties. The Company will decide, at its sole discretion, whether to file a Current Report on Form 8-K, and the MG Capital Parties may consider filing an amendment to its Schedule 13D.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the obligation to file required SEC documents promptly, contradicting securities regulations mandating timely disclosure of material events. By removing the 'shall' obligation and introducing discretion, the company risks non-compliance with securities laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and related SEC regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-2 requires the prompt filing of amendments to Schedule 13D for material changes. By giving the MG Capital Parties the option of whether to file, the modified text contradicts this requirement. The SEC mandates timely updates to ensure investors are informed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: this section, file an amendment on Schedule 13G ( § 240.13d-102 ) within five business days after the end of the first month in which the person's direct or indirect beneficial ownership, computed as of the last day of the month, increases or decreases by more than five percent of the class of equity securities. Once an amendment has been filed reflecting beneficial ownership of five percent or less of the class of securities, no additional filings are required by this paragraph (c) . ( d ) Any person relying on § 240.13d-1(c) that has filed its initial Schedule 13G ( § 240.13d-102 ) pursuant to § 240.13d-1(c) shall, in addition to filing any amendments pursuant to paragraph (b) of this section, file an amendment on Schedule 13G ( § 240.13d-102 ) within two business days after acquiring, directly or indirectly, greater than 10 percent of a class of equity securities specified in § 240.13d-1(d) , and thereafter within two business days after increasing or decreasing its beneficial ownership by more than five percent of the class of equity securities. Once an amendment has been filed reflecting beneficial ownership of five percent or less of the class of securities, no additional filings are required by this paragraph (d) . ( e ) The first electronic amendment to a paper format Schedule 13D ( § 240.13d-101 of this chapter ) or Schedule 13G ( § 240.13d-102 of this chapter ) shall restate the entire text of the Schedule 13D or 13G, but previously filed paper exhibits to such Schedules are not required to be restated electronically. See Rule 102 of Regulation S-T ( § 232.102 of this chapter ) regarding amendments to exhibits previously filed in paper format. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if the sole purpose of filing the first electronic Schedule 13D or 13G amendment is to report a change in beneficial ownership that would terminate the filer's obligation to report, the amendment need not include a restatement of the entire text of the Schedule being amended. Note to § 240.13 d -2: For persons filing a short-form statement pursuant to Rule 13d-1(b) or (c), see also Rules 13d-1(e), (f), and (g). perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Company and MG Capital Parties to file certain SEC documents. The changed text makes these filings optional, stating the Company 'may consider' issuing a press release and will decide 'at its sole discretion' whether to file a report, while the MG Capital Parties 'may consider' filing an amendment. This directly contradicts the requirement for timely updates to Schedule 13D, as explained in the Law Explanation and supported by the Perturbation Explanation that the change eliminates the obligation to file. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly state 'shall' in relation to 13D filings, the Law and Perturbation explanations make clear that this change introduces optionality for something that is required, hence the contradiction.
